questions.source_ref: Section 11.1: Three Types of Multiwinner Elections
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the three types of multiwinner elections introduced in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Excellence-based elections aim to select the $k$ best individuals independently
Diversity-based elections aim to cover different perspectives rather than simply pick the top $k$ similar candidates
Proportional representation aims to reflect voter groups proportionally
Excellence-based and diversity-based elections always produce the same committees when there are similar candidates
Parliamentary elections are given as an example of proportional representation

explanations.text: This is the definition given for excellence-based multiwinner elections.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.1: Three Types of Multiwinner Elections
explanations.notes_quote: Select the $k$ best individuals independently

explanations.text: The notes define diversity-based elections in terms of covering different perspectives.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.1: Three Types of Multiwinner Elections
explanations.notes_quote: Select a diverse committee covering different perspectives

explanations.text: This is the goal stated for proportional representation.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.1: Three Types of Multiwinner Elections
explanations.notes_quote: Reflect voter groups proportionally

explanations.text: The notes explicitly contrast these goals: two similar candidates may both be selected in excellence-based settings, but at most one may be selected in diversity-based settings.
Parliamentary elections are the example listed for proportional representation.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.1: Three Types of Multiwinner Elections
explanations.notes_quote: Parliamentary elections

questions.source_ref: Section 11.1: Key Differences from Single-Winner; Section 11.1: Challenges
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe key differences and challenges for multiwinner elections? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For two similar candidates, an excellence-based rule may select both, whereas a diversity-based rule may select at most one
The notes say rules suitable for shortlisting can produce poor parliaments, and vice versa
A major challenge is that many interesting multiwinner rules are NP-hard
The notes claim multiwinner rules are computationally easier than single-winner rules in almost all cases
Another challenge is understanding axiomatic properties to predict whether a rule suits an application

explanations.text: This is the explicit contrast drawn in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Two similar candidates: in excellence-based, select both (or neither); in diversity-based, select at most one

explanations.text: The notes state this directly to motivate why different goals need different rules.
explanations.notes_quote: Rules for shortlisting would produce poor parliaments and vice versa

explanations.text: Computational complexity is listed as one of the two main challenges.
explanations.notes_quote: Many interesting multiwinner rules are NP-hard

explanations.text: The notes say the opposite: many interesting multiwinner rules are NP-hard, unlike most single-winner rules.
Axiomatic analysis is the other main challenge listed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Axiomatic properties: How to predict if a rule suits a particular application?

questions.source_ref: Section 11.1: Formal Model; Section 11.1: Running Example
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the formal model and running example for multiwinner elections? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: An election is written as $(C, R)$ where $C$ is the candidate set and $R$ is the preference profile
In the approval model, each voter has an approval set $A_i \\subseteq C$
A multiwinner voting rule returns a nonempty family of size-$k$ subsets of $C$
In the running example, voter 6 approves $\\{b\\}$ only
The formal model requires $k > |C|$ so that ties can be broken by committee size

explanations.text: This is the formal notation introduced in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Election: $(C, R)$ where $C$ = candidates, $R$ = preference profile

explanations.text: This is the approval-model definition in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Approval (dichotomous) model: each voter $i$ has an approval set $A_i \\subseteq C$

explanations.text: The notes define the output of a multiwinner rule exactly this way.
explanations.notes_quote: returns nonempty family of size-$k$ subsets of $C$ (winning committees)

explanations.text: This is the approval ballot shown for voter 6 in the running example.
explanations.notes_quote: 6 | $b \\succ c \\succ d \\succ e \\succ a$ | $\\{b\\}$

explanations.text: The notes require $1 \\leq k \\leq |C|$, not $k > |C|$.

questions.source_ref: Section 11.1: Single Transferable Vote (STV)
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe STV? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: STV generalises single-winner IRV to committee selection
The quota is $q = \\lfloor n/(k+1) floor + 1$
If a candidate is ranked first by at least $q$ voters, they are elected and $q$ of their supporters are removed
If no candidate meets quota, STV elects all tied candidates immediately
For $k=1$, the quota becomes $\\lfloor n/2 floor + 1$, giving exactly IRV

explanations.text: This is how STV is introduced in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Generalises single-winner IRV to committee selection

explanations.text: This is the quota formula stated in the STV procedure.
explanations.notes_quote: Compute quota $q = \\lfloor n/(k+1) floor + 1$

explanations.text: This is the election step in each STV round.
explanations.notes_quote: If a candidate is ranked first by $\\geq q$ voters: elect them, remove $q$ of their supporters

explanations.text: If no candidate meets quota, STV eliminates the candidate with the fewest first-place votes.
The notes explicitly identify the $k=1$ case with IRV.
explanations.notes_quote: For $k=1$: $q = \\lfloor n/2 floor + 1$ (majority) -- this is exactly IRV

questions.source_ref: Section 11.1: Single Transferable Vote (STV)
questions.text:
Using the STV worked example with $k=2$ and $n=6$, which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

- Round 1: a=1, e=1, d=1, c=2, b=1
- Round 2: b=2, c=2, e=1, d=1
- Round 3: b=3 (voters 1,3,6)

Which statements are true? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The quota is $q = \\lfloor 6/3 floor + 1 = 3$
Candidate $a$ is eliminated first by tie-break in round 1
Candidate $b$ is elected in round 3 after reaching 3 first-place votes
After removing voters 1, 3, and 6, candidate $e$ is eliminated and then $c$ reaches 3 votes
The winning committee is $\\{A, C\\}$

explanations.text: This is the quota calculation shown in the example.
explanations.notes_quote: STV Example ($k=2$, $n=6$): $q = \\lfloor 6/3 floor + 1 = 3$

explanations.text: The notes say $a$ is eliminated first in round 1.
explanations.notes_quote: Round 1: a=1, e=1, d=1, c=2, b=1. No quota met. Eliminate a (tie-break).

explanations.text: This is the round-3 election step in the example.
explanations.notes_quote: Round 3: b=3 (voters 1,3,6). B elected.

explanations.text: This is exactly how the second seat is filled in the example.
explanations.notes_quote: Remaining: voter 2 (top: e), voters 4,5 (top: c). No quota. Eliminate e. C gets 3 votes (voters 2,4,5). C elected.

explanations.text: The winning committee in the example is $\\{B, C\\}$, not $\\{A, C\\}$.

questions.source_ref: Section 11.2: Committee Positions; Section 11.2: Committee Scoring Functions
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe committee positions and committee scoring functions? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For a size-$k$ committee $S$, $	ext{pos}_\\succ(S)$ is the sorted list of positions of the committee members in ranking $\\succ$
$[m]_k$ is the set of all increasing sequences of length $k$ from $\\{1, \\ldots, m\\}$
Position $I$ dominates $J$ if $i_t \\leq j_t$ for every $t$
A committee scoring function must assign lower scores to dominating positions to reward diversity
A committee scoring rule sums $\\gamma_{|C|,k}(	ext{pos}_{\\succ_i}(W))$ over all voters

explanations.text: This is the definition of committee position in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Position of $S$ in $\\succ$ = sorted list of positions of $S$'s members

explanations.text: This notation is defined explicitly in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: $[m]_k$ = set of all increasing sequences of length $k$ from $\\{1, \\ldots, m\\}$

explanations.text: This is the dominance relation on committee positions.
explanations.notes_quote: Position $I$ dominates $J$ if $i_t \\leq j_t$ for every $t$

explanations.text: The monotonicity condition is the opposite: if $I$ dominates $J$, then $\\gamma_{m,k}(I) \\geq \\gamma_{m,k}(J)$.
This is the score formula for committee scoring rules.
explanations.notes_quote: $$	ext{score}(W, E) = \\sum_{i=1}^{n} \\gamma_{|C|,k}(	ext{pos}_{\\succ_i}(W))$$

questions.source_ref: Section 11.2: Four Key Committee Scoring Rules
questions.text: Which of the following correctly match committee scoring rules to their scoring ideas? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: SNTV gives a point if the committee contains the voter's most preferred candidate
Bloc counts how many committee members appear in the voter's top-$k$ positions
$k$-Borda selects the $k$ candidates with highest individual Borda scores
Chamberlin-Courant uses the Borda score of the lowest-ranked committee member for each voter
Chamberlin-Courant cares only about the best representative for each voter

explanations.text: This is the interpretation of SNTV in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2: Four Key Committee Scoring Rules
explanations.notes_quote: Committee gets a point from voter if it contains their most preferred candidate

explanations.text: This is the Bloc interpretation in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2: Four Key Committee Scoring Rules
explanations.notes_quote: Each voter names their $k$ favourite candidates; winning committee = those mentioned most

explanations.text: This is exactly how $k$-Borda is described.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2: Four Key Committee Scoring Rules
explanations.notes_quote: Select $k$ candidates with highest individual Borda scores

explanations.text: Chamberlin-Courant uses the highest-ranked committee member, not the lowest-ranked one.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2: Four Key Committee Scoring Rules

explanations.text: This is the key intuition behind Chamberlin-Courant.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2: Four Key Committee Scoring Rules
explanations.notes_quote: Only best representative matters

questions.source_ref: Section 11.2: Four Key Committee Scoring Rules
questions.text: Which of the following correctly match the committee scoring formulas to the named rules? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\\gamma^{	ext{SNTV}}_{m,k}(i_1, \\ldots, i_k) = \\alpha_1(i_1)$ corresponds to SNTV
$\\gamma^{	ext{Bloc}}_{m,k}(i_1, \\ldots, i_k) = \\sum_{t=1}^{k} \\alpha_k(i_t)$ corresponds to Bloc
$\\gamma^{k	ext{-Borda}}_{m,k}(i_1, \\ldots, i_k) = \\sum_{t=1}^{k}  eta_m(i_t)$ corresponds to $k$-Borda
$\\gamma^{ eta	ext{-CC}}_{m,k}(i_1, \\ldots, i_k) =  eta_m(i_k)$ corresponds to Chamberlin-Courant
$\\gamma^{ eta	ext{-CC}}_{m,k}(i_1, \\ldots, i_k) =  eta_m(i_1)$ corresponds to Chamberlin-Courant

explanations.text: This is the SNTV formula in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2: Four Key Committee Scoring Rules
explanations.notes_quote: $$\\gamma^{	ext{SNTV}}_{m,k}(i_1, \\ldots, i_k) = \\alpha_1(i_1)$$

explanations.text: This is the Bloc formula in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2: Four Key Committee Scoring Rules
explanations.notes_quote: $$\\gamma^{	ext{Bloc}}_{m,k}(i_1, \\ldots, i_k) = \\sum_{t=1}^{k} \\alpha_k(i_t)$$

explanations.text: This is the $k$-Borda formula in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2: Four Key Committee Scoring Rules
explanations.notes_quote: $$\\gamma^{k	ext{-Borda}}_{m,k}(i_1, \\ldots, i_k) = \\sum_{t=1}^{k}  eta_m(i_t)$$

explanations.text: Chamberlin-Courant uses $ eta_m(i_1)$ because only the best representative matters, not the worst-ranked committee member.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2: Four Key Committee Scoring Rules

explanations.text: This is the Chamberlin-Courant formula in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2: Four Key Committee Scoring Rules
explanations.notes_quote: $$\\gamma^{ eta	ext{-CC}}_{m,k}(i_1, \\ldots, i_k) =  eta_m(i_1)$$

questions.source_ref: Section 11.2: Worked Example (k=2)
questions.text: Using the worked example for committee scoring rules with $k=2$, which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Under SNTV, candidate $C$ is ranked first by 2 voters, so every winning committee must contain $C$
Under Bloc, the top-2 appearance counts are $A=3$, $B=4$, $C=3$, $D=1$, $E=1$
Under Bloc, the winning committees are $\\{A,B\\}$ and $\\{B,C\\}$
Under $k$-Borda, the winner is $\\{A, C\\}$ because $A$ and $C$ have the two highest Borda scores
Under Chamberlin-Courant, $\\{A, C\\}$ beats $\\{B, C\\}$ because it scores 21 rather than 19

explanations.text: This is the SNTV conclusion in the worked example.
explanations.notes_quote: SNTV: C ranked first by 2 voters (most). Winning committee: $\\{C, x\\}$ where $x \\in \\{A, B, D, E\\}$

explanations.text: These are the Bloc counts listed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Bloc: Count appearances in top-2 positions: A=3, B=4, C=3, D=1, E=1

explanations.text: These are the winning committees under Bloc in the worked example.
explanations.notes_quote: Winning committees: $\\{A,B\\}$ or $\\{B,C\\}$

explanations.text: Under $k$-Borda, the notes give Borda scores $A=11$, $B=17$, $C=14$, $D=10$, $E=8$, so the winner is $\\{B, C\\}$.
This is the Chamberlin-Courant comparison given in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: $\\{B,C\\}$ scores 19. But $\\{A,C\\}$ scores 21. Winner: $\\{A, C\\}$

questions.source_ref: Section 11.3: Condorcet Committee (Fishburn, 1981); Section 11.3: Weak Condorcet Set (Gehrlein 1985, Ratliff 2003)
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Condorcet committees, weak Condorcet sets, and stability? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A Condorcet committee beats every other committee of the same size by majority preference
A weak Condorcet set requires that for every member $c$ and every non-member $d$, more than half the voters prefer $c$ to $d$
A multiwinner rule is stable if it outputs a weak Condorcet set whenever one exists
A weak Condorcet set is defined by comparing committees only through total Borda score
The Condorcet committee notion requires preferences over committees

explanations.text: This is the Fishburn definition in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Committee $C$ is a Condorcet committee if for every other committee $D$ of the same size, a majority of voters prefer $C$ to $D$

explanations.text: This is the definition of a weak Condorcet set in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: for every $c \\in S$ and every $d \\in C \\setminus S$: more than half the voters prefer $c$ to $d$

explanations.text: This is exactly how stability is defined.
explanations.notes_quote: A multiwinner rule is stable if it outputs a weak Condorcet set whenever one exists

explanations.text: Weak Condorcet sets are defined by pairwise majority comparisons between members and non-members, not by total Borda score.
The notes explicitly mention this requirement.
explanations.notes_quote: (Requires preferences over committees.)

questions.source_ref: Section 11.3: Stable Multiwinner Rules
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe NED and SEO? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Under NED, the score of a committee counts pairs $(c,d)$ with $c$ inside and $d$ outside the committee where at least half the voters prefer $c$ to $d$
Under SEO, the score is the minimum over $c \\in S$ and $d \\in C \\setminus S$ of the number of voters preferring $c$ to $d$
Both NED and SEO are presented as stable multiwinner rules
SEO maximises the average Borda score of committee members
For both NED and SEO, the highest score wins

explanations.text: This is the NED scoring definition in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Score of committee $S$ = number of pairs $(c, d)$ with $c \\in S$, $d \\in C \\setminus S$, where at least half the voters prefer $c$ to $d$

explanations.text: This is the SEO score formula in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: $$	ext{score}(S) = \\min_{c \\in S, d \\in C \\setminus S} |\\{i \\in N : c \\succ_i d\\}|$$

explanations.text: NED and SEO are introduced under the heading 'Stable Multiwinner Rules'.
explanations.notes_quote: Stable Multiwinner Rules

explanations.text: SEO is based on the weakest pairwise support of a committee member against a non-member, not on average Borda score.
The notes state this for both rules.
explanations.notes_quote: Highest score wins

questions.source_ref: Section 11.4: Setting; Section 11.4: w-AV Rules (Thiele, 1895)
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the approval-based setting and general $w$-AV framework? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Each voter $i$ submits an approval ballot $A_i \\subseteq C$
The utility of voter $i$ for committee $W$ is $u_i(W) = |A_i \\cap W|$
Given weights $w^{(k)}$, the voter-specific score is $\\sum_{j=1}^{|S \\cap A_i|} w_j^{(k)}$
A $w$-AV rule maximises the sum of these voter-specific scores over all voters
The $w$-AV framework applies only to ordinal ballots and cannot be used with approval ballots

explanations.text: This is the approval-based setting in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Each voter $i$ has approval ballot $A_i \\subseteq C$

explanations.text: This is the utility definition given in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Utility of voter $i$ for committee $W$: $u_i(W) = |A_i \\cap W|$

explanations.text: This is the general Thiele score formula.
This is how the winning committees are defined under $w$-AV.
explanations.notes_quote: The $w$-AV rule outputs committees maximising $\\sum_{i \\in N} 	ext{score}_i^{w^{(k)}}(S)$

explanations.text: The section is explicitly approval-based: it starts from approval ballots $A_i \\subseteq C$.

questions.source_ref: Section 11.4: Three Key Thiele Methods
questions.text: Which of the following correctly match the three key Thiele methods with their weight vectors and goals? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Approval Voting (AV) uses weights $(1,1,\\ldots,1)$ and aims at excellence / total approvals
Approval-based CC ($\\alpha$-CC) uses weights $(1,0,\\ldots,0)$ and aims at diversity / coverage
PAV uses weights $(1,1/2,1/3,\\ldots,1/k)$ and aims at proportional representation
AV uses harmonic weights and therefore captures diminishing returns
Under $\\alpha$-CC, only the first approved representative matters

explanations.text: This is the AV row in the Thiele-methods table.
explanations.notes_quote: Approval Voting (AV) | $(1, 1, \\ldots, 1)$ | ... | Excellence/total approvals

explanations.text: This is the $\\alpha$-CC row in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: Approval-based CC ($\\alpha$-CC) | $(1, 0, \\ldots, 0)$ | ... | Diversity/coverage

explanations.text: This is the PAV row in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: Proportional Approval Voting (PAV) | $(1, 1/2, 1/3, \\ldots, 1/k)$ | ... | Proportional representation

explanations.text: Harmonic weights belong to PAV, not AV. AV gives equal marginal utility for each additional representative.
This is the coverage intuition behind $\\alpha$-CC.
explanations.notes_quote: CC: only the first representative matters (coverage)

questions.source_ref: Section 11.4: Three Key Thiele Methods; Section 11.4: Intuition
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the score formulas and intuitions for AV, $\\alpha$-CC, and PAV? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: AV uses score $\\sum_{i \\in N} |A_i \\cap W|$

$\\alpha$-CC uses score $\\sum_{i \\in N} \\mathbb{1}_{A_i \\cap W 
eq \\emptyset}$
PAV uses score $\\sum_{i \\in N} H(|A_i \\cap W|)$ where $H(x)$ is the $x$-th harmonic number
Under PAV, the first approved representative is worth 1, the second 1/2, the third 1/3, and so on
Under AV, only the first approved representative matters and all later ones contribute zero

explanations.text: This is the AV score formula in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: $\\sum_{i \\in N} |A_i \\cap W|

explanations.text: This is the $\\alpha$-CC score formula in the table.
explanations.notes_quote:
$\\sum_{i \\in N} \\mathbb{1}_{A_i \\cap W 
eq \\emptyset}$

explanations.text: This is the PAV score formula in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: $\\sum_{i \\in N} H(|A_i \\cap W|)$

explanations.text: This is the diminishing-returns intuition for PAV.
explanations.notes_quote: PAV: diminishing returns -- first representative worth 1, second worth 1/2, third 1/3, etc.

explanations.text: That description matches $\\alpha$-CC, not AV. Under AV, each additional representative gives equal marginal utility.

questions.source_ref: Section 11.5: What is Proportionality?; Section 11.5: Justified Representation (JR)
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe proportionality and JR? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A cohesive group of at least $\\ell \\cdot n/k$ voters agreeing on at least $\\ell$ candidates deserves at least $\\ell$ seats
JR requires that if some group of at least $n/k$ voters unanimously approves a common candidate, then at least one member of that group must have a representative in the committee
JR can be written using the condition $|S| \\geq n/k$ and $| igcap_{i \\in S} A_i| \\geq 1$
JR requires every voter in the qualifying group to have utility at least 2
The notes interpret JR as guaranteeing at least one represented member for such a group

explanations.text: This is the proportionality principle stated at the start of the section.
explanations.notes_quote: If a cohesive group of $\\geq \\ell \\cdot n/k$ voters agrees on $\\geq \\ell$ candidates, they deserve $\\geq \\ell$ seats

explanations.text: This is the meaning of JR given in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: If a group of $\\geq n/k$ voters unanimously approves at least one common candidate, at least one member of this group must have a representative in the committee

explanations.text: These are the formal JR conditions in the definition.
explanations.notes_quote: whenever there exists $S \\subseteq N$ with $|S| \\geq n/k$ and $| igcap_{i \\in S} A_i| \\geq 1$

explanations.text: JR only requires that some member of the group has utility at least 1, not that every voter has utility at least 2.
This is the informal explanation given immediately after the formal definition.
explanations.notes_quote: at least one member of this group must have a representative in the committee

questions.source_ref: Section 11.5: Extended Justified Representation (EJR)
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe EJR? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: EJR quantifies over each $\\ell \\in [k]$
EJR requires that if a group of at least $\\ell \\cdot n/k$ voters shares at least $\\ell$ approved candidates, then some member of the group gets utility at least $\\ell$
EJR is strictly stronger than JR
JR is the special case of EJR with $\\ell = 1$
EJR is weaker than JR because it only applies when $\\ell > 1$

explanations.text: This is part of the formal EJR definition.
explanations.notes_quote: for each $\\ell \\in [k]$

explanations.text: This is the core EJR requirement in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: whenever there exists $S \\subseteq N$ with $|S| \\geq \\ell \\cdot n/k$ and $| igcap_{i \\in S} A_i| \\geq \\ell$, then $\\exists i \\in S$ such that $u_i(W) \\geq \\ell$

explanations.text: The notes state this relationship explicitly.
explanations.notes_quote: EJR is strictly stronger than JR

explanations.text: The notes identify JR as the $\\ell=1$ case of EJR.
explanations.notes_quote: JR = EJR with $\\ell = 1$

explanations.text: The notes say the opposite: EJR is strictly stronger than JR.

questions.source_ref: Section 11.5: Which Rules Satisfy JR/EJR?; Section 11.5: Theorem: CC satisfies JR.; Section 11.5: Theorem: PAV satisfies EJR.
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe which rules satisfy JR and EJR, and why? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Approval Voting satisfies neither JR nor EJR
Chamberlin-Courant satisfies JR but not EJR
PAV satisfies both JR and EJR
The CC proof idea is to replace a weakly covering committee member with the group's common approved candidate to improve the score
The PAV proof sketch uses a pigeonhole-style argument after adding a common approved candidate $c^*$

explanations.text: This is the AV row in the JR/EJR table.
explanations.notes_quote: Approval Voting (AV) | No | No

explanations.text: This is the CC row in the JR/EJR table.
explanations.notes_quote: Chamberlin-Courant (CC) | Yes | No

explanations.text: This is the PAV row in the JR/EJR table.
explanations.notes_quote: Proportional Approval Voting (PAV) | Yes | Yes

explanations.text: This is the contradiction argument in the CC proof.
explanations.notes_quote: Replace $c$ with the commonly approved candidate of $S$ -- this gives a higher CC score, contradicting optimality

explanations.text: This is the structure of the PAV proof sketch.
explanations.notes_quote: By pigeonhole, some $c'$ can be removed with loss $< n/k$, giving $(W \\cup \\{c^*\\}) \\setminus \\{c'\\}$ a higher PAV score

questions.source_ref: Section 11.5: Party-List Example
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the party-list example under PAV? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: There are 4 parties with 12 candidates each, so $m=48$
There are $n=22$ voters and $k=11$ seats
The supporter counts are $P_1:6$, $P_2:4$, $P_3:10$, $P_4:2$
The final PAV seat allocation is $P_1:3$, $P_2:2$, $P_3:5$, $P_4:1$
The proportionality check in the notes says a party deserves $\\ell$ seats whenever it has at least $\\ell \\cdot k/n$ supporters

explanations.text: This is the setup of the party-list example.
explanations.notes_quote: 4 parties ($P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$) with 12 candidates each ($m=48$)

explanations.text: These are the voter and seat counts in the example.
explanations.notes_quote: $n=22$ voters, $k=11$

explanations.text: These are the supporter counts listed in the notes.
This is the final allocation reported in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Final allocation: $P_1$: 3 seats, $P_2$: 2, $P_3$: 5, $P_4$: 1

explanations.text: The notes use the threshold $x \\geq \\ell \\cdot n/k = 2\\ell$, not $\\ell \\cdot k/n$.

questions.source_ref: Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT): Model
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the participatory budgeting model? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: There is a set $C$ of projects, each with cost $	ext{cost}(c) \\geq 0$
Each voter approves a subset $A_i \\subseteq C$ of projects
A feasible outcome $W$ must satisfy $\\sum_{c \\in W} 	ext{cost}(c) \\leq B$
If all costs equal 1 and $B$ is an integer, the model reduces to committee election
Participatory budgeting requires every approved project to be funded if it is approved by at least one voter

explanations.text: This is the first bullet in the participatory budgeting model.
explanations.notes_quote: Set $C$ of projects, each with cost $	ext{cost}(c) \\geq 0$

explanations.text: This is the voter model in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Set $N$ of $n$ voters, each approving $A_i \\subseteq C$

explanations.text: This is the feasibility constraint on the funded set.
explanations.notes_quote: Outcome: $W \\subseteq C$ with $\\sum_{c \\in W} 	ext{cost}(c) \\leq B$

explanations.text: The notes explicitly identify this special case with committee election.
explanations.notes_quote: If all costs = 1 and $B$ is integer, this reduces to committee election

explanations.text: The budget constraint prevents funding every approved project in general; the model only requires feasibility under total budget $B$.

questions.source_ref: Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT): Greedy Method
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the greedy method for participatory budgeting? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The method repeatedly picks the remaining project with the largest approval count
A chosen project is added to $W$ only if it is affordable
After considering a project, it is removed from the remaining set $R$
The notes interpret the method as approximately solving a knapsack problem
The greedy method always returns the exact optimal solution to the knapsack problem

explanations.text: This is the selection step in the greedy algorithm.
explanations.section_ref: Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT): Greedy Method
explanations.notes_quote: Pick $c^* = \\arg\\max_{c \\in R} |\\{i \\in N : c \\in A_i\\}|$ (most-approved project)

explanations.text: Affordability is checked before adding the project to the funded set.
explanations.section_ref: Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT): Greedy Method
explanations.notes_quote: If affordable: $W \\leftarrow W \\cup \\{c^*\\}$

explanations.text: This is the update step for the remaining projects.
explanations.section_ref: Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT): Greedy Method
explanations.notes_quote: $R \\leftarrow R \\setminus \\{c^*\\}$

explanations.text: The notes explicitly connect the greedy method to an approximate knapsack solution.
explanations.section_ref: Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT): Greedy Method
explanations.notes_quote: approximately solving a knapsack problem

explanations.text: The notes say the method approximately solves a knapsack problem; they do not claim exact optimality.
explanations.section_ref: Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT): Greedy Method

questions.source_ref: Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT): Greedy Method
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the utility interpretation and bang-per-buck discussion for participatory budgeting? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The greedy method orders projects by vote count
The notes say vote count equals bang-per-buck because $v_c \\cdot 	ext{cost}(c) / 	ext{cost}(c) = v_c$
The implied utility model is cost utility: $u_i(W) = 	ext{cost}(A_i \\cap W)$
The notes interpret the greedy method as maximising egalitarian welfare rather than utilitarian welfare
The payoffs in the approximate knapsack view are $v_c \\cdot 	ext{cost}(c)$

explanations.text: This is the first interpretation given after the algorithm.
explanations.section_ref: Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT): Greedy Method
explanations.notes_quote: The greedy method orders by vote count

explanations.text: This equality is written explicitly in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT): Greedy Method
explanations.notes_quote: vote count (= bang-per-buck since $v_c \\cdot 	ext{cost}(c) / 	ext{cost}(c) = v_c$)

explanations.text: This is the utility interpretation stated in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT): Greedy Method
explanations.notes_quote: This implies cities implicitly assume cost utilities: $u_i(W) = 	ext{cost}(A_i \\cap W)$

explanations.text: The notes connect the method to utilitarian cost-weighted welfare, not egalitarian welfare.
explanations.section_ref: Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT): Greedy Method

explanations.text: This is the payoff term used in the approximate knapsack interpretation.
explanations.section_ref: Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT): Greedy Method
explanations.notes_quote: approximately solving a knapsack problem with payoffs $v_c \\cdot 	ext{cost}(c)$

questions.source_ref: Section Key Definitions
questions.text: Which of the following correctly match multiwinner terms with their definitions from the key definitions table? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Multiwinner rule: maps an election and committee size $k$ to a set of size-$k$ committees
STV: iterative rule that elects candidates meeting quota $q = \\lfloor n/(k+1) floor + 1$ and eliminates the weakest
SNTV: score equals the number of voters whose top choice is in the committee
Bloc: score equals the Borda score of the committee's worst-ranked member
Chamberlin-Courant: score is the sum of each voter's best representative's Borda score

explanations.text: This is the first entry in the key definitions table.
explanations.notes_quote: Multiwinner rule | Maps election + committee size $k$ to set of size-$k$ committees

explanations.text: This is the STV entry in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: STV | Iterative: elect candidates meeting quota $q = \\lfloor n/(k+1) floor + 1$; eliminate weakest

explanations.text: This is the SNTV entry in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: SNTV | Score = voters whose top choice is in committee

explanations.text: Bloc is defined as total top-$k$ mentions, not as the Borda score of the worst-ranked member.
This is the Chamberlin-Courant entry in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: Chamberlin-Courant | Score = sum of each voter's best representative's Borda score

questions.source_ref: Section Key Definitions
questions.text: Which of the following correctly match proportionality and budgeting terms with their definitions from the key definitions table? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Condorcet set: every member beats every non-member pairwise by majority
NED / SEO: Condorcet-consistent multiwinner rules
AV: Thiele weights $(1,1,\\ldots)$ and maximises total approvals
PAV: Thiele weights $(1, 1/2, 1/3, \\ldots)$ and targets proportional representation
EJR: any group of at least $n/k$ with one common candidate must have every member represented

explanations.text: This is the Condorcet set entry in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: Condorcet set | Every member beats every non-member pairwise by majority

explanations.text: This is the NED / SEO entry in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: NED / SEO | Condorcet-consistent multiwinner rules

explanations.text: This is the AV entry in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: AV | Thiele weights $(1,1,\\ldots)$; maximise total approvals

explanations.text: This is the PAV entry in the table.
explanations.notes_quote: PAV | Thiele weights $(1, 1/2, 1/3, \\ldots)$; proportional representation

explanations.text: That statement describes JR informally, not EJR. EJR requires an $\\ell$-cohesive group and guarantees some member utility at least $\\ell$.

sections.title: Summary and Learning Objectives — Integrative Questions

questions.source_ref: Learning Objectives; Section 11.1 Multiwinner Elections; Section 11.2 Committee Scoring Rules; Section 11.4 Approval-Based Rules (Thiele Methods)
questions.text: Which of the following integrative statements correctly connect the main rule families and goals from Week 11? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Different multiwinner goals such as excellence, diversity, and proportional representation call for different rules
$k$-Borda is presented as capturing excellence, while Chamberlin-Courant captures diversity
Among the Thiele methods, AV targets excellence, $\\alpha$-CC targets diversity, and PAV targets proportionality
STV is presented as a committee version of IRV rather than as a Thiele method
The notes claim one universal rule works equally well for shortlisting, parliaments, and diversity-based selection

explanations.text: This is one of the main messages of the week.
explanations.notes_quote: Three goals: excellence, diversity, proportional representation -- different rules suit different goals

explanations.text: The summary explicitly contrasts these two committee scoring rules in this way.
explanations.notes_quote: CC captures diversity, $k$-Borda captures excellence

explanations.text: This is the mapping given in the Thiele-methods table and summary.
explanations.notes_quote: AV (excellence), $\\alpha$-CC (diversity), PAV (proportionality)

explanations.text: STV is introduced separately as the generalisation of IRV to committee selection.
explanations.notes_quote: Generalises single-winner IRV to committee selection

explanations.text: The notes explicitly reject this idea by saying rules for shortlisting would produce poor parliaments and vice versa.

questions.source_ref: Learning Objectives; Section 11.5 Proportional Representation; Section Participatory Budgeting (from LGT); Section Summary
questions.text: Which of the following integrative statements correctly summarise proportionality, representation guarantees, and budgeting from Week 11? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: PAV is highlighted as satisfying EJR and using harmonic weights
CC satisfies JR but not EJR
AV fails both JR and EJR
Participatory budgeting generalises committee elections by allowing projects with costs under a budget constraint
The summary says PAV is the unique sequence achieving proportional representation

explanations.text: This is stated both in the proportionality section and the summary.
explanations.notes_quote: PAV with harmonic weights $(1, 1/2, 1/3, \\ldots)$ is the unique sequence achieving proportional representation; it satisfies EJR

explanations.text: This is the relationship shown in the JR/EJR table.
explanations.notes_quote: Chamberlin-Courant (CC) | Yes | No

explanations.text: This is the AV row in the same table.
explanations.notes_quote: Approval Voting (AV) | No | No

explanations.text: This is the main modelling point of the participatory budgeting section.
explanations.notes_quote: Participatory budgeting generalises committee elections to projects with costs

explanations.text: This is stated explicitly in the summary.
explanations.notes_quote: PAV with harmonic weights $(1, 1/2, 1/3, \\ldots)$ is the unique sequence achieving proportional representation
